title: Critter Crawl: A Discussion and Review of Aftermath
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=St5jhqEeasY

Daniel (@dungeondive)'s formal review. Full disclosure up front — Plaid Hat sent the copy and he and Jerry Hawthorne speak daily. He still rips the rule book.

The headline verdict, despite that rip: Aftermath is **Jerry Hawthorne's best game since Mice and Mystics**, and likely his ultimate vision of what a *beast-epic dungeon crawl* should be. The encounter-card-references-storybook design is one of the smartest uses of a small card deck he has ever seen. The four-character-personal-goal campaign win condition gives the box genuine replay even without expansions. The tuck-box save-state design is so clean Daniel calls it player-friendly in a way the genre rarely manages. The Studio-Ghibli-meets-Redwall art lands without slipping into cute.

The rip is the rule book — and through it, a Plaid Hat trend that started with Comanauts. Too thin. Too few examples. No iconography reference. No flavour text. No connective narrative explaining how the mechanisms fit together. He finishes reading the manual and is *not* excited to play. He compares it unfavourably to Games Workshop's Shadows over Hammerhal, Warhammer Quest, and Blackstone Fortress — books that make him *want* to play their games. One specific in-game gripe: the roach swarm cleanup is anticlimactic and house-rule-worthy. Otherwise: a strong recommendation.

----

Daniel's central rule-book complaint is that a thin manual lands *flat* — you finish it without feeling excited to play. Have you ever read a thin rule book that *did* make you excited, and what did it do that the average Plaid Hat manual misses?
